optionList.content: “I will go get the client hot packs to alleviate the pain.”
“I will make sure not to give the client a water pitcher.”
“After completing vitals, I will notify you if the client has a fever.”
“When the client goes to the bathroom, I will keep track of the output.”
“I will go get the client hot packs to alleviate the pain.”
“I will make sure not to give the client a water pitcher.”
“After completing vitals, I will notify you if the client has a fever.”
“When the client goes to the bathroom, I will keep track of the output.”

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse and assistive personnel (AP) are providing care for a client with confirmed appendicitis. Which response by the assistive personnel indicates a need for further teaching?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Further teaching is indicated when the AP states that hot packs will be given to the client to alleviate pain. Applying heat to the appendix with appendicitis can cause increased circulation, resulting in more inflammation, rupture, and peritonitis. The client would be NPO in preparation of possible surgery; therefore, the client would not receive a water pitcher. The nurse needs to be notified if the client has a fever because fevers are indicative of further infection or peritonitis. It is important to keep track of all output to monitor fluid status.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is reviewing the etiology of peritonitis. Which of the following would the nurse identify as a chemical cause of peritonitis?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Peritonitis refers to a life-threatening, acute inflammation and infection of the parietal and visceral pleura of the peritoneal cavity. There are several bacterial pathogens that are responsible for peritonitis, including Streptococcus, Pneumococcus, or Escherichia coli (E. coli). Options 1, 2 and 3 are bacterial causes of peritonitis. Chemical causes of peritonitis include bile, pancreatic enzyme, or gastric acid leakage. Therefore, option 4 is correct, as bile leakage from the gallbladder is a chemical cause of peritonitis.

optionList.content: Calcification in the left upper quadrant (LUQ)
The presence of free air in the abdominal cavity
Calcified stones in the right upper quadrant (RUQ)
Shadowing at the junction of the kidney and the ureter
Calcification in the left upper quadrant (LUQ)
The presence of free air in the abdominal cavity
Calcified stones in the right upper quadrant (RUQ)
Shadowing at the junction of the kidney and the ureter

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is caring for a client being evaluated for peritonitis due to suspected gastric ulcer perforation. Which of the following findings on an abdominal x-ray supports this diagnosis?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Peritonitis refers to a life-threatening, acute inflammation and infection of the parietal and visceral pleura of the peritoneal cavity. There are several risk factors for peritonitis, including an internal cause by perforation, such as from appendicitis, diverticulitis, or peptic ulcer disease, or an external cause, such as a penetrating wound. When a gastric or duodenal ulcer perforates, free air and fluid leak into the peritoneal space. Therefore, x-ray imaging would demonstrate the presence of free air or fluid in the abdominal cavity. Eliminate option 1, because this could be indicative of pancreatic calcification. Eliminate option 3, because this could be indicative of cholelithiasis. Next, eliminate option 4 because this could indicate nephrolithiasis. Therefore, option 2 is correct, as peritonitis resulting from gastric or duodenal ulcer rupture would demonstrate either free air or fluid in the abdominal cavity.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is caring for a client who presented to the emergency department with a sudden onset of sharp, severe abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, anorexia, and a rigid and board-like abdomen. The nurse notes that the client has a history of peptic ulcer disease (PUD). Which of the following conditions would the nurse suspect?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Peritonitis refers to a life-threatening, acute inflammation and infection of the parietal and visceral pleura of the peritoneal cavity. There are several risk factors for peritonitis, including an internal cause by perforation, such as from appendicitis, diverticulitis, or peptic ulcer disease, or an external cause, such as a penetrating wound. Noting the client data in the question, peritonitis is likely due to the sudden onset of sharp and severe abdominal pain with a rigid and board-like abdomen, in addition to the gastrointestinal symptoms of nausea, vomiting, and anorexia. Peptic ulcer disease can increase the risk of peritonitis, which can occur as a complication of a ruptured peptic ulcer. When a peptic ulcer ruptures, gastric contents can leak into the peritoneal space, which can cause subsequent infection and inflammation. Therefore, option 2 is correct.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is caring for a client with diagnosed appendicitis that is awaiting surgical intervention scheduled the next day. In which of the following positions would it be best for the nurse to place the client?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Appendicitis is acute inflammation of the appendix. If the client is able to tolerate it, the optimal position in which to place the client diagnosed with appendicitis is the semi-Fowler’s position, as this contains abdominal drainage in the lower abdomen. Therefore, option 3 is correct.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is reviewing the laboratory results of a client diagnosed with peritonitis. Which of the following laboratory results supports this diagnosis?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Peritonitis refers to a life-threatening, acute inflammation and infection of the parietal and visceral pleura of the peritoneal cavity. Laboratory findings associated with peritonitis include leukocytosis, abnormal renal function labs, and fluid and electrolyte imbalances. Renal dysfunction and fluid and electrolyte imbalances result from massive vasodilation that causes fluid third spacing into the peritoneal cavity related to inadequate circulating volume. Eliminate option 1, because this is a normal potassium level; a normal potassium level ranges from 3.5-5.0 mEq/L (3.5-5.0 mmol/L). Eliminate option 2, because this is a normal white blood cell (WBC) count; a normal WBC count ranges from 5,000 to 10,000/mm3 (5-10 × 109/L). Next, eliminate option 3 as a blood urea nitrogen (BUN) of 10 mg/dL (3.6 mmol/L) is within normal limits; a normal BUN ranges from 10-20 mg/dL (3.6-7.1 mmol/L). Note that option 4 indicates leukocytosis and is the correct answer.

optionList.content: Apply a cold pack to the abdomen.
Administer 30 mL of milk of magnesia (MOM).
Initiate an intravenous (IV) line for the administration of IV fluids.
Apply a cold pack to the abdomen.
Administer 30 mL of milk of magnesia (MOM).
Initiate an intravenous (IV) line for the administration of IV fluids.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client arrives at the hospital emergency department complaining of acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain, and appendicitis is suspected. Laboratory tests are performed, and the nurse notes that the client's white blood cell (WBC) count is elevated. On the basis of these findings, the nurse would question which primary health care provider (PHCP) prescription documented in the client's medical record?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Appendicitis would be suspected in a client with an elevated WBC count complaining of acute right lower abdominal quadrant pain. Laxatives are never prescribed because if appendicitis is present, the effect of the laxative may cause a rupture with resultant peritonitis. Cold packs may be prescribed for comfort. The client would be NPO and given IV fluids in preparation for possible surgery.

optionList.content: Halfway between the umbilicus and left iliac crest
Halfway between the umbilicus and right iliac crest
Halfway between the left ischial tuberosity and iliac crest
Halfway between the right ischial tuberosity and right iliac crest
Halfway between the umbilicus and left iliac crest
Halfway between the umbilicus and right iliac crest
Halfway between the left ischial tuberosity and iliac crest
Halfway between the right ischial tuberosity and right iliac crest

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is performing a gastrointestinal (GI) assessment on a client with suspected appendicitis. The nurse assesses for pain at McBurney’s point, knowing this landmark is located where on the abdomen?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Appendicitis is acute inflammation of the appendix. A common cause is the development and subsequent obstruction of the appendix lumen by a fecalith, which is a mass of accumulated feces. A common presenting symptom is dull periumbilical pain that shifts to the right lower quadrant and then localizes at McBurney’s point. McBurney’s point is located halfway between the umbilicus and right iliac crest. Therefore, option 2 is correct. Remember that the appendix is anatomically located in the right lower quadrant to assist in answering the question correctly.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client comes into the emergency department with suspected appendicitis. The nurse places the client in which position that would be best for the client?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
The client would be placed in a semi-Fowler’s position because this position promotes the containment of abdominal drainage in the lower abdomen. A supine position is a flat position, and a Trendelenburg position is one in which the client is flat with the legs elevated and the head lowered. This will not promote containment of abdominal drainage in the lower abdomen. The high-Fowler’s position will increase abdominal pressure and cause increased discomfort for the client.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is caring for a client diagnosed with peritonitis who is receiving intravenous (IV) fluids and antibiotics who is awaiting surgery. In which of the following positions would the nurse place the client?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Peritonitis refers to a life-threatening, acute inflammation and infection of the parietal and visceral pleura of the peritoneal cavity. Placing the client in a Semi-Fowler’s position promotes the drainage of peritoneal fluid into the distal region of the abdominal cavity, which assists in mitigating complications. Therefore, option 2 is correct, as the client positions in options 1, 3, and 4 would not promote drainage of peritoneal fluid into the distal region of the abdominal cavity.

optionList.content: Call and ask the operating room team to perform surgery as soon as possible.
Reposition the client and apply a heating pad on the warm setting to the client's abdomen.
Call and ask the operating room team to perform surgery as soon as possible.
Reposition the client and apply a heating pad on the warm setting to the client's abdomen.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is monitoring a client admitted to the hospital with a diagnosis of appendicitis who is scheduled for surgery in 2 hours. The client begins to complain of increased abdominal pain and begins to vomit. On assessment, the nurse notes that the abdomen is distended and bowel sounds are diminished. Which is the most appropriate nursing intervention?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
On the basis of the signs and symptoms presented in the question, the nurse would suspect peritonitis and notify the surgeon. Administering pain medication is not an appropriate intervention. Heat would never be applied to the abdomen of a client with suspected appendicitis because of the risk of rupture. Scheduling surgical time is not within the scope of nursing practice, although the surgeon probably would perform the surgery earlier than the prescheduled time.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client presents with severe abdominal pain, and appendicitis is suspected. On assessment, the nurse would expect localized tenderness in which abdominal area in the later stages of appendicitis?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
McBurney’s point is located midway between the umbilicus and the anterior iliac crest in the right lower quadrant. Throughout the later stages of appendicitis, McBurney’s point is the area in which localized tenderness would be noted on assessment.

optionList.content: White blood cell (WBC) count of 4000 mm3 (4 × 109/L)
White blood cell (WBC) count of 4000 mm3 (4 × 109/L)

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is caring for a client admitted to the hospital with suspected acute appendicitis. Which laboratory result would the nurse expect to note if the client does have appendicitis?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Laboratory findings do not establish the diagnosis of appendicitis, but there is often a moderate elevation of the WBC count (leukocytosis) to 10,000 to 18,000 mm3 (10 to 18 × 109/L) with an increased number of immature WBCs. An inflammatory process causes a rise in the WBC count. A rise to 26,000 mm3 (26 × 109/L) may indicate a perforated appendix (greater than 20,000 mm3 [20 × 109/L]).

optionList.content: A young client who is nauseous and vomiting.
A client with advanced dementia who is pulling at his nasogastric tube.
An elderly client who states is constipated and cannot move the bowels.
A client who is tachycardic, febrile, and has severe abdominal pain in the right lower quadrant.
A young client who is nauseous and vomiting.
A client with advanced dementia who is pulling at his nasogastric tube.
An elderly client who states is constipated and cannot move the bowels.
A client who is tachycardic, febrile, and has severe abdominal pain in the right lower quadrant.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse has received the client assignment for the day. Which client would the nurse care for first?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
The nurse would first assess the client in option 4, because tachycardia, fever, and right lower quadrant pain can be caused by an acute abdomen such as appendicitis, or even perforation of the appendix that can result in peritonitis. Next, the nurse would assess the client with dementia in option 2, because pulling at a nasogastric tube is a safety priority concern. Then, the nurse would assess the client who is nauseous and vomiting, because fluid and electrolyte imbalance is a concern. Lastly, the nurse would care for the elderly client who is constipated, because this is not an immediate priority at this time.

studentQuestionArray.content: The emergency department nurse is assessing a client presenting with right lower quadrant pain, low-grade fever, nausea, vomiting and anorexia. The client explains to the nurse that the abdominal pain was dull at first and in the middle of the abdomen but is now sharp and midway between the client's right hip and umbilicus. Physical exam reveals abdominal muscle guarding and rebound tenderness. Which of the following conditions would the nurse suspect?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Appendicitis is acute inflammation of the appendix. Typically, the pain of appendicitis begins with dull periumbilical pain that then moves and localizes to the right lower quadrant. Other signs and symptoms include a low-grade fever, nausea, vomiting, anorexia, abdominal rigidity, rebound tenderness, and muscle guarding. Eliminate option 1, because hepatitis would typically result in right upper quadrant pain. Next, eliminate option 3, because cholecystitis would result in right upper quadrant pain with possible radiation to the shoulder. Finally, eliminate option 4, because pain from nephrolithiasis is typically colicky in nature and located in the flank area. Therefore, option 2 is correct.

studentQuestionArray.content: The emergency department nurse is assessing a client with suspected appendicitis with complaints of right lower quadrant pain, nausea, vomiting, and anorexia. Which diet orders would the nurse anticipate?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Appendicitis is acute inflammation of the appendix. A common cause is the development and subsequent obstruction of the appendix lumen by a fecalith, which is a mass of accumulated feces. Surgery is indicated to remove the inflamed appendix before it ruptures, which can result in life-threatening peritonitis. Therefore, the client would need to be placed on NPO status to prepare for possible surgical intervention. Therefore, option 4 is correct.

optionList.content: Maintain on NPO (nothing by mouth) status.
Apply a heating pad to the lower abdomen for comfort.
Initiate an intravenous (IV) line with the administration of IV fluids.
Maintain on NPO (nothing by mouth) status.
Apply a heating pad to the lower abdomen for comfort.
Initiate an intravenous (IV) line with the administration of IV fluids.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client arrives at the hospital emergency department complaining of acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain. Appendicitis is suspected, and appropriate laboratory tests are performed. The emergency department nurse reviews the test results and notes that the client's white blood cell (WBC) count is elevated. The nurse also reviews the prescriptions from the primary health care provider (PHCP). The nurse would contact the PHCP to question which order if noted in the client's record?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Appendicitis would be suspected in a client with an elevated WBC count who is complaining of acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain. A semi-Fowler's position is maintained for comfort. The client would be on NPO status and given IV fluids in preparation for possible surgery. Heat would never be applied to the abdomen because this may increase circulation to the appendix, potentially leading to increased inflammation and perforation.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client with appendicitis is scheduled for an appendectomy. The nurse providing preoperative teaching for the client describes the location of the appendix by stating that it is attached to which part of the gastrointestinal (GI) system?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
The appendix, sometimes referred to as the vermiform appendix, is attached to the apex of the cecum. The other locations listed are incorrect.

studentQuestionArray.content: A nurse is assigned to care for a client with peritonitis. The nurse reviews the client’s medical record, recalling that which are potential causes of peritonitis? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Peritonitis occurs when there is inflammation of the peritoneum and the endothelial lining of the abdominal cavity. Primary causes of peritonitis are from blood-borne organisms. This occurs in clients with cirrhosis with ascites, and genital tract organisms. Secondary causes of peritonitis occur when organs perforate and leak contents into the peritoneal cavity. This includes a ruptured appendix, blunt trauma to abdomen, ruptured diverticulosis, ischemic bowel disorders, pancreatitis, peritoneal dialysis, or perforated peptic ulcers or intestines.

studentQuestionArray.content: A nurse is planning care for a client with suspected peritonitis. What diagnostic studies would the nurse expect to most likely be performed? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Peritonitis is the inflammation of the peritoneum and endothelial lining of the abdominal cavity. The primary health care provider would order an abdominal x-ray to analyze whether dilated loops, bowel obstruction, perforation, or paralytic ileus has occurred. A CT scan and ultrasound can be used to identify abscesses and ascites. A peritoneoscopy can be used in clients without ascites to visualize the peritoneum and biopsy if needed. An electroencephalogram (EEG) is used to examine brain activity through electrical impulses and would not be indicated for this client. A PET scan measures the metabolic activity of body tissues and organs and would not be needed for this client.

studentQuestionArray.content: The emergency department nurse is assessing a client with suspected appendicitis. Which signs and symptoms of appendicitis might be noted in the assessment? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Appendicitis is acute inflammation of the appendix. Typically, the pain of appendicitis begins with dull periumbilical pain that then moves and localizes to the right lower quadrant. Other signs and symptoms include a low-grade fever, nausea, vomiting, anorexia, abdominal rigidity, rebound tenderness, and muscle guarding. Eliminate option 3 because anorexia, or decreased appetite, is associated with appendicitis, not polyphagia, or excessive hunger. Next, eliminate option 4 because right lower quadrant pain, not left lower quadrant pain, is associated with appendicitis. Therefore, options 1, 2, 5, and 6 are correct.

optionList.content: Heart rate of 58 beats per minute
Heart rate of 58 beats per minute

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is assessing a client for signs and symptoms of peritonitis. Which of the following signs or symptoms would alert the nurse to the possibility of this condition? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Peritonitis refers to a life-threatening, acute inflammation and infection of the parietal and visceral pleura of the peritoneal cavity. There are several risk factors for peritonitis, including an internal cause by perforation, such as from appendicitis, diverticulitis, or peptic ulcer disease, or an external cause, such as a penetrating wound. Left untreated, fluid volume shifts into the peritoneal space due to blood vessel dilation, resulting in third spacing and ultimately inadequate tissue perfusion. Signs and symptoms of peritonitis include sudden onset of severe abdominal pain, fever, abdominal distention, nausea and vomiting, tachycardia, decreased urine output, hiccups, and inability to pass flatus or feces. Eliminate option 2 because decreased urine output, not polyuria or excessive urine output, is a sign of peritonitis. Next, eliminate option 4 because this temperature is within normal limits and an elevated temperature is a sign of peritonitis. Eliminate option 6 because tachycardia, not bradycardia, is a sign of peritonitis. Therefore, options 1, 3 and 5 are correct.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is planning to implement the prescriptions for an assigned client with confirmed appendicitis. Which prescription(s) would the nurse question? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Appendicitis is the acute inflammation of the appendix. Polyethylene glycol, a laxative, and a saline enema are both used to cleanse the bowels. A client who has confirmed or suspected appendicitis would not receive laxatives or enemas because perforation of the appendix can occur. Once appendicitis is confirmed, as prescribed, the nurse would administer antibiotics, opioid analgesics, and intravenous fluids to provide comfort and care to the client. Ampicillin is a class of penicillin antibiotics used intravenously to protect against infection, and normal saline would be used intravenously to maintain fluid and electrolyte balance. Ketorolac is an NSAID analgesic, and morphine is an opioid analgesic. Both are used to alleviate pain for the client.

optionList.content: Heart rate of 68 beats per minute
Reports sudden sharp pain in the midepigastric region
Heart rate of 68 beats per minute
Reports sudden sharp pain in the midepigastric region

studentQuestionArray.content: A client in the emergency department is suspected of having peritonitis. Upon assessment, which findings would the nurse expect to note? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Peritonitis occurs when a localized inflammation is present throughout the peritoneum and endothelial lining of the abdominal cavity. Clinical manifestations include fever, abdominal distention, tachycardia, nausea, vomiting, tachypnea, and altered bowel patterns. A client with peritonitis would be tachypneic with shallow breaths because of the pain. To help decrease the tension on the abdominal muscles, the client will assume the fetal position. Sudden sharp pain is present as the pain correlates to the amount and type of gastrointestinal contents spilled. Hypoventilation occurs when a reduced amount of air enters the lungs, leading to decreased oxygen.
